content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 2, 2008

William G. Brennan
Executive Director
New Motor Vehicle Board
1507 21st Street, Suite 300
Sacramento, California 95811

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-144

Dear Mr. Brennan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do the provisions of Regulation 18944.2, as recently revised, apply to the New Motor Vehicle Board's use of a conference room provided by a party to a pending action for mandatory settlement conferences or other proceedings?

CONCLUSION

No. A conference room provided by an outside party for use by the New Motor Vehicle Board in holding its proceedings does not provide a personal benefit to an official of the agency and is therefore not a gift under the Act.

FACTS²

In 2006, the New Motor Vehicle Board (the "Board") requested advice on whether it could accept offers from litigants to use their conference rooms for mandatory settlement conferences that were currently being held only in state offices. Pursuant to your request, we advised that "[i]f the requirements of regulation 18944.2 are met, the board may accept these

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Additionally facts were provided in our telephone conversation of August 22, 2008, in which one of your attorneys, Polly Riggenbach, provided further background information.

File No. A-08-144
Page No. 2

offers and no individual board member will be considered to have received a gift. The gift
will be considered made to the agency and not its members." (Weinberg Advice Letter, I-06-
032)

Since that time, the Board has been using litigants' offices space and/or meeting
rooms for purposes of presiding over mandatory settlement conferences, merit hearings, and
law and motion hearings while complying with the requirements of former Regulation
18944.2 as advised by Commission staff. You would now like to know if, in light of the
recent changes to Regulation 18944.2, the revised section applies to the Board's use of
conference rooms provided by parties to a pending action. You have indicated that the parties
must first stipulate to the use of a conference room provided by one of the parties before the
Board will agree to its use.

ANALYSIS

Section 82028 defines gift as ". . . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . ."
Regulation 18944.2, as amended, applies to situations where an agency receives something
of value from an outside source, and it sets forth the conditions where it will not "be
considered a reportable gift [under the Act] to an individual public official, . . .
although the official receives a personal benefit from the payment." (Emphasis added.) In
short, Regulation 18944.2 applies only to payments that meet the Act's definition of gift
(those that provide a personal benefit to a public official).

Under the facts you have presented, there is nothing to indicate that a "personal
benefit" will be received by any Board official in his or her use of the conference room. The
benefit, it appears, is being provided to the agency in that by receiving use of the room the
agency will not have to search to obtain similar space in another building and may even
avoid the cost of renting space at some other facility. The officials who participate in each
proceeding, we assume, will always be provided with a room in which to perform their
duties. The fact that their participation will take place in a room provided by an outside
source rather than in a room acquired from state resources or with state funds does not
provide a personal benefit to them. Our advice is limited to the actual use of the conference
room itself and does not include any items that may be tangential to its use, such as food or
beverages, which clearly do provide a personal benefit and would be considered gifts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William J. Kenkelt
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl
